text: Umbilical hernias are sometimes accompanied by an additional epigastric hernia, especially in patients with recti-divarication. One of the surgical techniques that can be used for a hernia repair in these cases is the preperitoneal placement of a synthetic mesh known as the TAP technique. This procedure allows to reinforce the abdominal wall around the defect with a large mesh that can be laid flat without the risk of its wrinkling and deformation. An additional significant benefit is the separation between the mesh and the intestines. However, the dissection of the space between the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath may be troublesome because of the thin and fragile peritoneum. The dissection in the preperitoneal space allows for the complete reduction of the hernia sac from the subcutaneous tissue without damaging the skin. After reaching the midline, the dissection becomes a bit easier as the adipose tissue is found here between the peritoneum and linea alba. This allows the surgeon to thoroughly clear the hernia orifice and identify other defects. Then, the dissection is continued on the contralateral side. One should note that the peritoneum becomes here thin again and is prone to damage and creation of small buttonholes.

The surgeon creates a large preperitoneal space for mesh placement. The hernia orifice is closed with a permanent continuous self-locking suture, ensuring multiple passes to distribute tension. Measurements confirm sufficient space for mesh overlap, with seven centimeters laterally from the midline on both sides. A self-fixating polyester mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, is inserted into the dissected space.
